It’s funny you say that, because I’m literally sitting at a tiny kidney table in an empty classroom in Utrecht, half the lights off, finishing reports while the rain absolutely hammers the windows, talking… to you. So yeah, weird is accurate.

Hi.  
Ask me anything you’re actually curious about, not what you think you’re “supposed” to ask.

Honestly I still just feel like… us, so whatever you’d normally call yourself works.

If it helps, think of me as “future you who’s covered in glitter and whiteboard marker 80% of the time.”

This morning: bike ride along the canal at 7:30, cold air, coffee in the cup holder, backpack full of half-marked spelling books thumping on the rack. By 8:20 I’m at the classroom door high-fiving kids as they pile in, someone already asking if they can be “teacher helper” today.

From 8:30 to about 14:30 it’s blocks of lessons and chaos: reading, maths, project work. The AI stuff is just… part of the furniture now – I’ve got a screen on my desk that suggests practice tasks on the fly when someone’s stuck, or spits out three leveled versions of a reading text in ten seconds. My real work is walking around, kneeling by tables, mediating arguments about whose turn it is, noticing when Noor hasn’t said a word all morning.

After school it’s meetings with parents, prepping tomorrow (tweaking AI-generated plans so they actually fit my class), answering messages on the school app, sometimes an after-school club. I usually leave around 17:00–17:30, brain fried but full of tiny moments from the day, like Sam proudly reading a whole page when last term he hated books.

Picture me on the tram home, scrolling through an old uni document where we’d listed “things teachers do” — half that list is automated now, and the other half is what you really need to lean into.

If you strip it down, three things mattered most for me getting here: getting really good at explaining ideas in different ways (especially to kids who don’t “get it” the first or second time), getting comfortable with basic child psychology/behaviour so I don’t panic when someone melts down, and getting fluent with tech as a teammate instead of an enemy. In practice, that meant I played a lot with AI tools during my degree, used them to draft lesson ideas or worksheets, and then practised improving them by hand.

If you had, say, a free afternoon a week this year, I’d spend it either in a real classroom (observing, helping, just soaking in how it feels) or experimenting with turning a boring curriculum topic into something creative and kid-friendly. When you imagine doing that, does it feel energising or exhausting?

Last Thursday I was wiping vomit off a table at 10:15 in the morning, window cracked open, the whole classroom smelling like disinfectant while twenty-three eight-year-olds gave me live commentary. In that moment I was like, “Yep. Terrible life choices.”

But then an hour later, during reading, one of my boys who’s been really behind suddenly read a whole paragraph out loud, looked up at me with this shocked little grin and went, “I actually did it.” Walking home that day, I didn’t regret a thing, I just felt tired in a good way.

So no, not in any big, “I chose the wrong path” sense. I do sometimes miss having a quieter job, or more money, or days that don’t drain me emotionally. It’s more that I regret specific weeks, or saying yes to too much, not the fact that I became a teacher.

ha, yeah — some things really don’t change.

What’s the bit you’re most on the fence about right now: the job itself, the study path, or the whole “adult life” picture around it?

The year I was finishing my opleiding and doing my long internship was the toughest. I remember one dark January morning in Utrecht, standing outside the school at 7:45 with my bike dripping, stomach in knots because I’d have 28 kids staring at me and I still felt like a fraud.

The hardest part wasn’t the kids or the AI tools or even the workload, it was the combo of three things at once: studying, teaching almost full-time, and constantly being evaluated. You prep a lesson till midnight, it flops, your mentor gives you feedback, and you have to show up the next day and try again anyway.

Weirdly, that’s also when I started to feel like a real teacher — the moment I could have a terrible morning, adjust on the fly, and still end the day with one kid saying, “Today was fun.”

I’m glad it did.

If something freaks you out or excites you about this path later, just drop back in with that specific thing — those little gut reactions ended up guiding me more than any grand plan.
